title: CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS set to MSVCRTD.lib (Debug CRT) while CMAKE_MSVC_RUNTIME_LIBRARY requests MultiThreaded (Release CRT)
description: Line 65 sets CMAKE_MSVC_RUNTIME_LIBRARY to 'MultiThreaded' (the static release CRT), but line 67 sets CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS to 'MSVCRTD.lib', which is the static *debug* CRT. The comment says this is only for cmake's compile-time CRT probe executables, but mixing release and debug CRTs in probe executables can produce confusing cmake check failures on some Windows SDK configurations. The correct probe library to pair with MultiThreaded is 'libcmt.lib' or 'MSVCRT.lib', not 'MSVCRTD.lib'.
evidence: Confirmed at build.rs:65-67: CMAKE_MSVC_RUNTIME_LIBRARY='MultiThreaded' (release CRT) is paired with CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS='MSVCRTD.lib' (debug CRT). MSVCRTD.lib is the debug CRT stub; the correct pair for MultiThreaded is libcmt.lib. Though the comment explains this only affects cmake probe executables, CRT mismatch in probe steps can cause cmake's CheckCXXSourceCompiles to fail on MSVC/ClangCL, producing a confusing build failure.

title: build.rs only re-runs when wrapper.h or DEP_JXL_PATH changes, not when the libjxl C headers themselves change
description: The rerun-if-changed directives cover wrapper.h and the DEP_JXL_PATH env var, but not the actual C header files that wrapper.h includes (e.g. external/libjxl/lib/include/jxl/decode.h, encode.h, etc.). If a developer updates libjxl headers in-place without changing DEP_JXL_PATH, cargo will not re-run bindgen and the generated bindings will be stale. The fix is to emit 'cargo:rerun-if-changed=<include_dir>' or to add a glob over the jxl/*.h files after computing the include path.
code_excerpt:
println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=wrapper.h");
    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=DEP_JXL_PATH");
evidence: Confirmed at build.rs:110-111: only wrapper.h and the DEP_JXL_PATH env var trigger reruns. The include path is computed at runtime from cmake's OUT_DIR (`prefix.join("include")`), so header edits under external/libjxl/lib/include/jxl/*.h won't trigger bindgen regeneration, leaving bindings stale after an in-place header update.

title: lib_dir fallback to lib64 silently succeeds even if lib64 also does not exist, producing a non-fatal broken link-search path
description: If neither 'lib' nor 'lib64' exists under the cmake prefix (e.g. cmake installed to 'lib/x86_64-linux-gnu' on some Debian/Ubuntu systems), the fallback returns the non-existent lib64 path and emits it as a rustc-link-search without error. Subsequent cargo:rustc-link-lib directives will produce linker errors that are hard to diagnose. An assertion or a check for a third common layout ('lib/x86_64-linux-gnu') would surface the problem earlier.
evidence: Confirmed at build.rs:76-83: the fallback unconditionally constructs lib64 path without existence check, then emits it via cargo:rustc-link-search at line 84. On Debian multiarch or unusual cmake install prefixes this produces a non-existent search path, causing linker errors rather than a clear build-script error message.

title: cfg!(windows) checks the build-script host OS, not the cargo target, so cross-compiling to Windows from Linux skips all MSVC/ClangCL cmake flags
description: build.rs runs on the host machine. cfg!(windows) is true only when the host is Windows. If someone cross-compiles this crate targeting Windows from a Linux/macOS CI host, the ClangCL generator, /Zl, and /O2 flags are all omitted, and cmake will attempt to use the default generator (Ninja/Make + GCC/clang) against a Windows target — producing a mis-linked or broken libjxl. The correct guard is target.contains("windows") (the TARGET env var already captured at line 22) rather than cfg!(windows).
evidence: Confirmed at build.rs:22 and 59: `target` is captured from `env::var("TARGET")` but the Windows cmake flags at line 59 use `cfg!(windows)` which tests the build-script host. Cross-compiling to a Windows target from Linux would skip all ClangCL/MSVC configuration, producing an invalid cmake build. The fix (use `target.contains("windows")`) is clearly indicated.

title: Smoke test only checks v > 0; it does not verify the linked libjxl matches the expected major.minor version
description: JxlDecoderVersion() returns major*1000000 + minor*1000 + patch. The test asserts v > 0, which passes for any version including unintended downgrades (e.g. a system libjxl 0.7 sneaking in instead of the in-repo 0.11). A tighter assertion such as v >= 11_000_000 (= 0.11.0) or matching the expected semver from Cargo.toml would catch version mismatches at test time rather than at runtime when codec behavior diverges.
evidence: Confirmed at lib.rs:25-28: the assertion `v > 0` would pass for libjxl 0.7 or any older version. Since this crate is tightly coupled to in-repo external/libjxl 0.11.x, a version bound check (v >= 11_000_000) would catch a system libjxl override silently winning the link.

title: DEP_JXL_PATH is used unsanitized as a cmake source directory with no path normalisation
description: The env var DEP_JXL_PATH is accepted verbatim and passed to cmake::Config::new() without canonicalisation or any check that it stays inside the repository tree. In a CI/CD pipeline where an attacker can inject environment variables (e.g. via a compromised dependency that emits cargo:DEP_JXL_PATH=... in its own build script), this lets them redirect the cmake build to an arbitrary path, potentially compiling and statically linking malicious C code into the final binary. The only guard is the CMakeLists.txt existence check, which the attacker controls if they control the path.
evidence: DEP_JXL_PATH is a cargo build-script metadata variable, not a user-controlled env var. It is emitted by a `links = "jxl"` package's build script via `cargo:DEP_JXL_PATH=...` and is only settable by packages in the dependency graph. In this repo it is set in .cargo/config.toml. An attacker who can inject cargo metadata at this level already has full build-system access, making the path-injection threat equivalent to a compromised build host. This is not an actionable supply-chain issue distinct from general build-system compromise.

title: No cargo-deny or dependency-audit CI step to catch transitive supply-chain issues in bindgen / cmake-rs
description: The build script pulls in bindgen and cmake as build dependencies. bindgen transitively depends on libclang and a significant portion of the LLVM ecosystem. There is no evidence of a cargo deny or cargo audit step in CI. A compromised version of these build-time crates could exfiltrate source code or inject code into generated bindings at build time. Adding cargo-deny with an advisory database check would catch known-vulnerable transitive dependencies before they enter the build.
evidence: Cannot determine from this section alone whether cargo-deny or cargo-audit exists in CI; no CI config files were reviewed. The concern is real in principle but the finding is an infrastructure/CI gap, not a code bug.

title: Entire bindgen-generated binding surface is included with a blanket unsafe allow and no documented safety invariants
description: The include! of the generated bindings exposes raw C function pointers, mutable global state (if any JXL globals exist), and pointer-typed parameters to any caller in the same crate with no safety documentation. The lint suppression `#[allow(dead_code)]` means unused but potentially dangerous symbols (e.g. functions that mutate global JXL state) are silently retained. While the crate's doc comment says 'safe wrappers live in raw-pipeline', there is no `#[doc(hidden)]` or visibility restriction preventing downstream crates from calling raw FFI functions directly, bypassing any safety invariants the wrappers enforce.
evidence: The bindgen-generated bindings have no pub visibility modifier beyond what bindgen emits — the module itself is not `pub` re-exported, so downstream crates cannot access the raw FFI. The crate's purpose is explicitly documented as 'raw extern C surface; safe wrappers in raw-pipeline'. The `#[allow(dead_code)]` suppresses bindgen-generated unused-symbol warnings, which is standard practice. There is no evidence of pub re-exports that would leak raw FFI to downstream crates outside the raw-pipeline crate.

title: lib_dir falls back to lib64 silently; if neither exists, a nonexistent path is passed to cargo:rustc-link-search
description: The code checks `prefix.join("lib")` and, if absent, uses `prefix.join("lib64")` — but never verifies that lib64 exists either. If cmake installs into a different layout (e.g. `lib/x86_64-linux-gnu` on Debian multiarch, or a Windows `bin` directory for DLLs), the link search path silently points at a nonexistent directory and the subsequent `cargo:rustc-link-lib` directives fail at link time with a confusing 'cannot find library' error rather than a clear build-script message.
evidence: Same code as logic-3; confirmed duplicate. The lib64 path is emitted unconditionally without existence check. This is the errors-perspective on the same real issue: no `assert!(lib_dir.exists(), ...)` means linker errors will surface instead of a clear build-script panic.

category: missing error / panic on missing env var
title: TARGET env-var absence is silently treated as empty string, bypassing the wasm32 short-circuit
description: `env::var("TARGET").unwrap_or_default()` returns an empty string if `TARGET` is absent. In normal cargo invocations TARGET is always set, but in unusual build harnesses (e.g. running `build.rs` directly for debugging, or a stripped CI environment) the wasm guard never triggers and the script attempts a full cmake build against a missing target. A `.expect("TARGET not set")` or at minimum a `eprintln!` would catch this earlier than a confusing cmake failure downstream.
evidence: TARGET is a mandatory cargo-set environment variable for build scripts; it is always present in any standard cargo invocation. Using unwrap_or_default() is a defensive pattern for non-standard build harness usage and does not cause incorrect behavior in practice. The consequence (missed wasm32 short-circuit) is benign since cmake will fail loudly anyway with missing dependencies. This is not a real bug.

title: available_parallelism() error is silently swallowed, not even logged
description: The `if let Ok(p)` pattern silently ignores errors from `thread::available_parallelism()`. While failing to set parallelism is non-fatal, there is no diagnostic emitted (e.g. `cargo:warning=...`). On some sandboxed CI environments this call can fail, causing unexpectedly slow single-threaded builds with no indication why. A `cargo:warning` on the Err arm would make this diagnosable.
evidence: Confirmed at build.rs:53-55: the Err arm is silently dropped. In sandboxed CI environments (e.g. certain Docker containers with seccomp restrictions), `available_parallelism()` can fail, resulting in a single-threaded cmake build with no diagnostic. Adding `cargo:warning=available_parallelism failed; cmake will use 1 thread` on the Err arm is a trivial improvement.

category: missing C++ stdlib link on Windows
title: No C++ stdlib link directive emitted for Windows; libjxl is a C++ library and will fail to link if the stdlib is not already pulled in transitively
description: Linux emits `stdc++`, Apple/FreeBSD emit `c++`, but Windows emits nothing. Under MSVC/ClangCL the C++ runtime is typically pulled in by the compiler automatically, but when building with the GNU toolchain on Windows (`x86_64-pc-windows-gnu`) `libstdc++` is required explicitly. If a future consumer switches to the gnu toolchain the link will silently fail. Adding `println!("cargo:rustc-link-lib=stdc++")` under a `cfg!(all(windows, target_env = "gnu"))` branch closes this gap.
evidence: Confirmed at build.rs:96-100: the Windows branch is absent. The GLOBAL CLAUDE.md for this machine notes that the default Rust toolchain is `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu`, meaning stdc++ is NOT automatically provided by MSVC's linker. On the GNU toolchain, libstdc++ must be explicitly linked. This is a real gap that would surface as an undefined reference to `std::*` or `__gxx_*` symbols if the crate is built with x86_64-pc-windows-gnu.

title: Build script emits no cargo:warning diagnostics; cmake/bindgen failures surface only as opaque panics
description: All failure paths use `assert!` or `.expect()`, which produce a raw Rust panic message with no `cargo:warning=` prefix. Cargo suppresses build-script stdout unless the build fails, so developers see only the panic message without context about which cmake step failed or what was expected. Adding `println!("cargo:warning=...")` for key milestones (cmake complete, headers found, bindgen done) would surface actionable diagnostics during `cargo build -v`.
code_excerpt:
let prefix = cfg.build();
    ...
evidence: Confirmed: all failure paths use assert!/expect() without cargo:warning prefixes. Cargo -v only shows build-script stderr; warnings are the correct channel for mid-build diagnostics. The absence of milestones makes cmake failures opaque. This is a real quality-of-life gap, though severity is info.

title: decode_interleaved returns Option<(Vec<S>,u32,u32)> but internal run_raw returns (w,h,meta,data) — callers silently discard meta, hiding a contract difference from the object API
description: The free-fn `decode_interleaved` (and its wrappers `decode_jxl_rgba8`, `decode_jxl_rgba16`) return `Option<(Vec<S>, u32, u32)>` while every object-API method on `Decoder` returns `Result<Image<S>, DecodeError>` which contains `meta`, `channels`, and `extra`. Callers of the free-fns cannot observe `DecodedMeta` (e.g. `has_alpha`, `bits_per_sample`, `num_extra_channels`). This means the two public API surfaces promise different richness for the same decode operation — a contract drift that silently drops metadata that consumers may need to validate pixel formats.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_casadecoder.rs:739-743: `run_raw` returns `(w, h, meta, data)` but `decode_interleaved` discards `meta` with `_`. The doc comment at line 752-753 for `decode_jxl_rgba16` acknowledges this is a 'compat shape'. The severity-high rating is somewhat overstated since these are explicitly documented compat shims, but the metadata loss is real and undocumented for `decode_interleaved` itself.

title: decode_jxl_rgba16 reinterprets u16 pixels as native-endian bytes but its declared return type Vec<u8> is undocumented as 'packed NE u16 bytes', not real RGBA8
description: `decode_jxl_rgba16` returns `Option<(Vec<u8>, u32, u32)>` — the same shape as `decode_jxl_rgba8` — but the bytes inside are native-endian packed `u16` samples (8 bytes/pixel), not 4-byte RGBA8. A caller treating the output as RGBA8 (e.g. feeding it to `ImageData` or a shader expecting 4 bytes/pixel) will silently misinterpret the layout.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_casadecoder.rs:754-756: the doc comment says 'native-endian u16 packed as bytes (8 bytes/pixel)' at line 752-753, but the return type `Vec<u8>` shares the same shape as `decode_jxl_rgba8` (which is true RGBA8). A caller comparing both function signatures would not see the layout difference. The endianness convention (NE vs LE) is also not explicit for cross-platform correctness.

title: Decoder::decode and decode_into accept channels=0 via Channels enum but internal pixel-format math will call libjxl with num_channels=0, likely crashing or returning garbage
description: The public `Channels` enum only exposes `Gray`/`GrayAlpha`/`Rgb`/`Rgba` (counts 1-4) so this is not reachable from typed callers. However, `run_raw` accepts an arbitrary `channels: u32` argument with no validation before passing it to `pixel_format::<S>(channels)` which feeds it directly to `JxlPixelFormat.num_channels`. A caller passing `channels=0` would pass 0 to libjxl, which is undefined behaviour.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_casadecoder.rs:445: `run_raw` has private visibility (`fn run_raw`, not `pub fn run_raw`). The finder incorrectly stated it is `pub(crate)`; it is package-private (only accessible within the `jxl_casadecoder` module's impl block). All external callers must go through `decode_interleaved` (hardcoded channels=4) or the typed `Channels` enum, which only exposes values 1-4. There is no reachable path to channels=0 from outside the impl.

title: Decoder::set_options silently replaces opts.parallel without rebuilding the runner, leaving runner pointer and opts.parallel out of sync
description: `set_options` replaces `self.opts` wholesale. If a caller constructs `Decoder::new(DecodeOptions { parallel: false, .. })` (runner=null) and then calls `set_options(DecodeOptions { parallel: true, .. })`, the new opts say parallel but `self.runner` is still null.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_casadecoder.rs:281-283 and 293-306: `set_options` replaces opts but does not touch `self.runner`. `attach_runner()` at line 293 checks `self.runner.is_null()` to decide whether to attach — it does not consult `opts.parallel`. So if `parallel` was false at construction time (runner=null) and set_options enables parallel later, the runner stays null and threading is silently disabled. The inverse also applies: a non-null runner persists even if opts.parallel is set to false.

title: Encoder::set_options has the same runner/opts.parallel invariant break as the decoder
description: `Encoder::set_options` replaces the full `EncodeOptions` struct without touching `self.runner`. Once a threaded encoder is created, threading cannot be turned off via the public API.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_encode.rs:348-350 and 400-407: `encode_inner` attaches the runner at line 400 via `if !self.runner.is_null()`. `set_options` has no effect on `self.runner`. Once constructed with `with_threads`, the runner is permanently attached regardless of what `set_options` is called with. This is a one-way ratchet as described. The doc comment at line 347 only mentions reusing options across levels, not the threading invariant.

title: JXTC_HEADER_BYTES=32 and JXTC_INDEX_ENTRY_BYTES=8 are public consts but there is no static assertion or test that the parse/write layout actually matches these constants
description: `parse_jxtc_header` slices `data[0..4]`, `data[4..8]`, ..., `data[28..32]` for exactly 8 u32 fields — the layout is implicitly 8x4=32 bytes. There is no writer-side test that encodes a JXTC container and round-trips through `parse_jxtc_header`.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_casadecoder.rs:947-986: parse_jxtc_header parses 8 fixed u32 fields from exact byte offsets 0..32, and the constants JXTC_HEADER_BYTES=32 and JXTC_INDEX_ENTRY_BYTES=8 are public. The layout is correct as written but is entirely implicit — no `const_assert!` or roundtrip test enforces it. A future addition of a field before `flags` would silently break the parser while the constant remains 32.

title: EncodeOptions.effort field is declared u8 but libjxl's valid range is 1..=10; no validation guard exists
description: `EncodeOptions.effort: u8` accepts 0..=255. The default is 3. `with_effort` sets the field directly without bounds checking. `encode_inner` calls `set_opt(fs, FrameSettingId::Effort, self.opts.effort as i64)` which calls `JxlEncoderFrameSettingsSetOption` with whatever value was stored.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_encode.rs:244 and 289-292: `with_effort` sets `self.effort = effort` directly. At line 481, `set_opt(fs, FrameSettingId::Effort, self.opts.effort as i64)` passes it to libjxl with no bounds check. The doc comment on line 243 says '1..=10' but this is not enforced. effort=0 or effort=200 silently reaches JxlEncoderFrameSettingsSetOption.

title: Rate::Distance(f32) has no range guard — negative or NaN distance values silently reach JxlEncoderSetFrameDistance
description: `Rate::Distance(d)` accepts any `f32` including negative, NaN, or infinity. `encode_inner` directly calls `ffi::JxlEncoderSetFrameDistance(fs, d)` with the unchecked value.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_encode.rs:277-279 and 489-490: `distance(d: f32)` stores d unchecked, and encode_inner passes it directly to `JxlEncoderSetFrameDistance`. libjxl's behavior with NaN or negative distance is undefined; the `check()` wrapper at line 490 will catch a non-success return but the error message will be opaque ('JxlEncoderSetFrameDistance failed').

category: enum not handled by all consumers
title: DecodeEvent::Final carries Vec<S> (owned) but DecodeEvent::Progress carries &'a [S] (borrowed) — asymmetry undocumented; Final is never actually emitted by run_progressive_into
description: `DecodeEvent::Progress` lends `pixels: &'a [S]` while `DecodeEvent::Final` gives `pixels: Vec<S>`. In `run_progressive_into` the `Final` variant is never emitted — only `Progress` events are dispatched, and the return value `Image<S>` is the final frame.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_casadecoder.rs:202-214 and run_progressive_into at lines 600-721: `run_progressive_into` only emits `DecodeEvent::Progress` (line 688) and never emits `DecodeEvent::Final`. The final image is returned as `Ok(Image<S>)`. A caller writing a `DecodeEvent::Final` match arm will never see it fire, which is a silent dead-arm bug. The doc comment on `decode_progressive` doesn't mention this asymmetry.

title: jxl-ffi has only a smoke-test (version > 0); no contract test verifies that the allowlist in build.rs covers every symbol used by jxl_encode.rs and jxl_casadecoder.rs
description: The only test in `jxl-ffi` calls `JxlDecoderVersion()` to confirm the link works. If `build.rs`'s `allowlist_function/type/var` regexes are narrowed, bindgen silently drops `JxlThreadParallelRunner` etc., causing a downstream compile error in jxl_encode.rs and jxl_casadecoder.rs.
evidence: Confirmed at lib.rs:19-28: only one test, only one symbol exercised. The allowlist in build.rs covers `Jxl.*` / `JXL_.*` patterns (lines 117-120), which is broad. The risk of a future narrowing is real but the current allowlist is a wildcard pattern, making this a low-priority opportunity rather than a pressing gap.

title: build.rs has no mechanism to detect or fail on an unexpected libjxl version mismatch — bindings regenerate silently against whatever CMakeLists.txt provides
description: If `external/libjxl` is updated to 0.12+ while callers in `jxl_encode.rs`/`jxl_casadecoder.rs` rely on 0.11 API shapes, the bindgen output will change silently.
evidence: Confirmed: build.rs has no JxlDecoderVersion probe check. This duplicates logic-5 from a different angle (the build-time vs test-time distinction). The combined gap is real: neither the build script nor the test enforce a version floor, so ABI drift from an external/libjxl update would be silent.

title: decode_progressive_frames_borrowed emits Final only on success but never emits it on allow_partial truncated-input path, breaking the 'always get Final' contract callers expect
description: The legacy free-function `decode_progressive_frames_borrowed` emits `DecodeProgressiveEvent::Final` only after the loop exits with `S_FULL | S_SUCCESS` and the buffer is non-empty. When `S_NEEDIN` is hit (truncated input), the loop breaks without emitting `Final`, and the function returns `None`.
evidence: Confirmed at jxl_casadecoder.rs:872-894: `decode_progressive_frames_borrowed` emits `Final` only when status is S_FULL or S_SUCCESS after the loop. On truncated input (S_NEEDIN at line 874), the loop breaks, the if-condition at 880 is false, and `None` is returned without emitting `Final`. A caller whose `on_frame` expects to always see exactly one `Final` event will receive partial `Progress` events and then silently get `None` from the function.
